section.title: The First Journey: Cyprus and Pisidian Antioch
section.sectionRationale: The gospel now moves out on purpose. The Spirit sets apart Barnabas and Saul at Antioch, and the first missionary journey begins. On Cyprus a sorcerer is silenced and a proconsul believes; at Pisidian Antioch Paul preaches his first recorded sermon, tracing Israel's story to Jesus, the risen Savior in whom sins are forgiven and sinners justified. When many Jews reject the message, Paul and Barnabas turn openly to the Gentiles, and the word spreads through the whole region.
section.thesis: The Holy Spirit sends the church into the world, and through Paul's proclamation the crucified and risen Jesus is announced as the promised Savior in whom there is forgiveness and a justification the law could never give, a message that divides Israel and opens wide to the Gentiles.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the commissioning at Antioch; Cyprus and the blinding of Elymas; Paul's synagogue sermon in Pisidian Antioch, from the exodus to the resurrection to the call to believe; and the Gentiles' joy set against rising opposition.

units.label: Set apart by the Spirit at Antioch
units.note: the church sends its best into mission

verses.text: Now in the church at Antioch there were prophets and teachers: Barnabas, Simeon called Niger, Lucius of Cyrene, Manaen (who had been brought up with Herod the tetrarch), and Saul.
While they were worshiping the Lord and fasting, the Holy Spirit said, “{{aphorizo|Set apart}} for Me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them.”
And after they had fasted and prayed, they laid their hands on them and sent them off.

units.label: On Cyprus: Elymas silenced, a proconsul believes
units.note: the gospel confronts sorcery and wins

verses.text: So Barnabas and Saul, sent forth by the Holy Spirit, went down to Seleucia and sailed from there to Cyprus.
When they arrived at Salamis, they proclaimed the word of God in the Jewish synagogues. And John was with them as their helper.
They traveled through the whole island as far as Paphos, where they found a Jewish sorcerer and false prophet named Bar-Jesus,
an attendant of the proconsul, Sergius Paulus. The proconsul, a man of intelligence, summoned Barnabas and Saul because he wanted to hear the word of God.
But Elymas the sorcerer (for that is what his name means) opposed them and tried to turn the proconsul from the faith.
Then Saul, who was also called Paul, filled with the Holy Spirit, looked directly at Elymas
and said, “O child of the devil and enemy of all righteousness, you are full of all kinds of deceit and trickery! Will you never stop perverting the straight ways of the Lord?

Now look, the hand of the Lord is against you, and for a time you will be blind and unable to see the light of the sun.” Immediately mist and darkness came over him, and he groped about, seeking someone to lead him by the hand.
When the proconsul saw what had happened, he believed, for he was astonished at the teaching about the Lord.

units.label: The sermon: Israel's story leads to Jesus
units.note: from the exodus to David to the promised Savior

verses.text: After setting sail from Paphos, Paul and his companions came to Perga in Pamphylia, where John left them to return to Jerusalem.
And from Perga, they traveled inland to Pisidian Antioch, where they entered the synagogue on the Sabbath and sat down.
After the reading from the Law and the Prophets, the synagogue leaders sent word to them: “Brothers, if you have a word of encouragement for the people, please speak.”
Paul stood up, motioned with his hand, and began to speak: “Men of Israel and you Gentiles who fear God, listen to me!
The God of the people of Israel chose our fathers. He made them into a great people during their stay in Egypt, and with an uplifted arm He led them out of that land.
He endured their conduct for about forty years in the wilderness.
And having vanquished seven nations in Canaan, He gave their land to His people as an inheritance.
All this took about 450 years. After this, God gave them judges until the time of Samuel the prophet.
Then the people asked for a king, and God gave them Saul son of Kish, from the tribe of Benjamin, who ruled forty years.
After removing Saul, He raised up David as their king and testified about him: ‘I have found David son of Jesse a man after My own heart; he will carry out My will in its entirety.’
From the descendants of this man, God has brought to Israel the Savior Jesus, as He promised.

units.label: The resurrection and the offer of forgiveness
units.note: raised, never to see decay; justified through Him

verses.text: Before the arrival of Jesus, John preached a baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel.
As John was completing his course, he said, ‘Who do you suppose I am? I am not that One. But there is One coming after me whose sandals I am not worthy to untie.’
Brothers, children of Abraham, and you Gentiles who fear God, it is to us that this message of salvation has been sent.
The people of Jerusalem and their rulers did not recognize Jesus, yet in condemning Him they fulfilled the words of the prophets that are read every Sabbath.
And though they found no ground for a death sentence, they asked Pilate to have Him executed.
When they had carried out all that was written about Him, they took Him down from the tree and laid Him in a tomb.
But God raised Him from the dead,
and for many days He was seen by those who had accompanied Him from Galilee to Jerusalem. They are now His witnesses to our people.
And now we proclaim to you the {{euangelizo|good news}}: What God promised our fathers
He has fulfilled for us, their children, by raising up Jesus. As it is written in the second Psalm: ‘You are My Son; today I have become Your Father.’
In fact, God raised Him from the dead, never to see decay. As He has said: ‘I will give you the holy and sure blessings promised to David.’
So also, He says in another Psalm: ‘You will not let Your Holy One see decay.’
For when David had served God’s purpose in his own generation, he fell asleep. His body was buried with his fathers and saw decay.
But the One whom God raised from the dead did not see decay.
Therefore let it be known to you, brothers, that through Jesus the {{aphesis|forgiveness}} of sins is proclaimed to you.
Through Him everyone who believes is {{dikaioo|justified}} from everything from which you could not be justified by the law of Moses.
Watch out, then, that what was spoken by the prophets does not happen to you:
‘Look, you scoffers, wonder and perish! For I am doing a work in your days that you would never believe, even if someone told you.’ ”

units.label: Gentile joy and rising opposition
units.note: a light for the Gentiles, salvation to the ends of the earth

verses.text: As Paul and Barnabas were leaving the synagogue, the people urged them to continue this message on the next Sabbath.
After the synagogue was dismissed, many of the Jews and devout converts to Judaism followed Paul and Barnabas, who spoke to them and urged them to continue in the grace of God.
On the following Sabbath, nearly the whole city gathered to hear the word of the Lord.
But when the Jews saw the crowds, they were filled with jealousy, and they blasphemously contradicted what Paul was saying.

Then Paul and Barnabas answered them boldly: “It was necessary to speak the word of God to you first. But since you reject it and do not consider yourselves worthy of eternal life, we now turn to the Gentiles.
For this is what the Lord has commanded us: ‘I have made you a light for the Gentiles, to bring salvation to the ends of the earth.’ ”
When the Gentiles heard this, they rejoiced and glorified the word of the Lord, and all who were appointed for eternal life believed.
And the word of the Lord spread throughout that region.
The Jews, however, incited the religious women of prominence and the leading men of the city. They stirred up persecution against Paul and Barnabas and drove them out of their district.
So they shook the dust off their feet in protest against them and went to Iconium.
And the disciples were filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit.

aphorizo.gloss: To mark off, set apart, dedicate for a purpose. The Spirit sets apart Barnabas and Saul for the mission. The same root lies behind Paul's description of himself as set apart for the gospel, Romans 1:1; the sending of the church begins with God's own claim.

euangelizo.gloss: To announce good news, to preach the gospel. Paul's whole sermon is this word in action: the promise made to the fathers has been fulfilled in the raising of Jesus. The good news is not advice but the announcement of what God has already done.

aphesis.gloss: Release, pardon, forgiveness; the letting go of a debt. Through Jesus the forgiveness of sins is proclaimed. It is the heart of the offer: not that hearers must earn release, but that release is announced to them in the risen Christ.

dikaioo.gloss: To justify, to declare righteous, to acquit. Everyone who believes is justified from everything the law of Moses could not justify them from. Here in Paul's first sermon is the seed of the doctrine unfolded in Romans and Galatians: a right standing given through faith in Christ, not achieved by law.

basic.context: The church at Antioch, rich with prophets and teachers, is worshiping and fasting when the Holy Spirit sets apart its two strongest leaders, Barnabas and Saul, for a mission. They sail first to Cyprus, where a sorcerer named Elymas tries to keep a Roman official from the faith and is struck blind, and the official believes. Then they reach Pisidian Antioch, where in the synagogue Paul preaches his first recorded sermon, walking through Israel's history to arrive at Jesus, crucified, risen, and offered as the Savior in whom sins are forgiven. Many Jews reject the message, so Paul and Barnabas turn to the Gentiles, who receive it with joy.
basic.hermeneutics: Follow the sermon's shape: God's saving acts in Israel's past all lead to Jesus. Paul preaches the resurrection as the fulfilment of God's promises and then presses the application, that in Christ there is a forgiveness and a justification the law could never provide. Notice the pattern that will repeat through Acts: the gospel goes to the Jew first, provokes division, and opens to the Gentiles, and the word keeps spreading.
basic.originalLanguages: Key words carry the passage. The Spirit says to set apart, aphorizo, Barnabas and Saul. Paul announces the good news, euangelizo, that God kept His promise by raising Jesus. And he presses home two gifts: aphesis, the forgiveness of sins, and dikaioo, to be justified, declared righteous, which everyone who believes receives but the law of Moses could never give.
basic.typology: David is the pattern and the pointer. God found in David a man after His own heart, yet David died and saw decay. Jesus, David's greater son, is raised never to see decay, inheriting the sure mercies promised to David. The exodus God worked with an uplifted arm foreshadows the greater rescue announced in the risen Christ.
basic.christ: Christ is the whole burden of the sermon. The story of Israel narrows to Him: the promised Savior from David's line, crucified as the prophets foretold, raised by God never to see decay, and now proclaimed so that all who believe are forgiven and justified. The resurrection is central, proven from Psalm 2 and Psalm 16, and it is the ground of the offer of salvation.
basic.god: God is the one who acts throughout: He chose the fathers, led Israel out, gave David, kept His promise, and raised Jesus from the dead. The mission itself is God's initiative through the Spirit. And God's saving purpose reaches beyond Israel: He has appointed His servant to be a light for the Gentiles and salvation to the ends of the earth.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 2:7 and Psalm 16:10 are quoted to prove the resurrection of the Messiah. Isaiah 49:6 supplies the commission to be a light for the Gentiles. Habakkuk 1:5 warns the scoffers who reject the word. Romans 4 and Galatians 2 unfold the justification by faith that Paul first announces here.
basic.oneStory: This is the launch of the mission that carries Acts forward: the ascended Christ, by His Spirit, sends the church out, and the gospel begins its journey from Antioch toward the ends of the earth. Paul's sermon gathers up the whole Old Testament story and shows it flowing to Jesus, and the turning to the Gentiles fulfils the ancient promise that God's salvation would reach every nation.

context.literary: Acts 13 opens the second half of Acts, in which Paul becomes the central figure and the gospel moves into the Gentile world. Paul's sermon at Pisidian Antioch is the fullest recorded example of his synagogue preaching, structured like Peter's earlier sermons: a survey of God's acts, the announcement of Jesus crucified and risen, proof from the Psalms, and a call to respond.
context.historical: The journey, dated around AD 47 to 48, runs from Syrian Antioch to Cyprus, Barnabas's homeland, and then into the highlands of Asia Minor. Sergius Paulus was a real Roman proconsul; synagogues across the empire drew God-fearing Gentiles, which is why Paul's message finds a ready Gentile hearing there.
context.canonical: The sermon binds the Testaments, reading Israel's history and the Psalms as pointing to the risen Christ, and it plants the doctrine of justification by faith that Romans and Galatians will develop. The commission from Isaiah 49:6 shows the Gentile mission as the fulfilment of Scripture, not a departure from it.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the Spirit-directed launch of the Gentile mission and to give a model of apostolic preaching: Israel's whole story leads to Jesus, crucified and risen, in whom forgiveness and justification are offered to all who believe, Jew and Gentile alike.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes how the first journey unfolded and how the synagogue responded; it does not prescribe blinding opponents or shaking off dust as a formula. What abides is the gospel Paul preaches, the resurrection of Jesus and justification by faith, and the pattern of taking that gospel to all peoples.

debates.question: What does justified from everything the law could not justify, verse 39, mean for the place of the law?

views.label: The law never justified; Christ alone justifies
views.case: The strongest reading is that the law of Moses could deal with certain matters but could never give a righteous standing before God; only through faith in the risen Christ is a person justified from all things. This anticipates Paul's argument in Romans and Galatians that righteousness comes by faith, not by works of law.

views.label: Christ completes what the law began
views.case: Others stress continuity, that the law pointed forward and its sacrifices anticipated Christ, so that justification in Christ fulfils rather than merely replaces the law. Rightly held this honours the law's God-given role as a signpost, though it must not blunt Paul's point that the law could not justify at all.

debates.bearing: Held well, the verse guards the good news that a full and free acquittal is found only in Christ, received by faith, while still honouring the law as God's true word that pointed to Him.

study.originalLanguages: The passage turns on a chain of key words. The Spirit commands the church to set apart, aphorizo, its leaders, so the mission is God's claim before it is the church's plan. In the synagogue Paul announces the good news, euangelizo, that the promise to the fathers is kept in the raising of Jesus. And he presses the double gift secured by the resurrection: aphesis, the release and forgiveness of sins, and dikaioo, to be justified, acquitted, declared righteous, which everyone who believes receives through Christ and no one could receive through the law.

typology.fulfillment: The rescue of Israel from Egypt foreshadows the greater salvation announced in the risen Christ, God's mighty act to deliver a people to Himself.

typology.type: David, a man after God's heart
typology.fulfillment: David served his generation and saw decay; his greater Son Jesus is raised never to see decay and receives the sure mercies promised to David, Psalm 16 and Isaiah 55:3.

typology.type: A light for the Gentiles, Isaiah 49:6
typology.fulfillment: The servant's calling to bring salvation to the ends of the earth is fulfilled as the gospel turns to the Gentiles, who rejoice and believe.

study.christ: The sermon is Christ from first to last. He is the promised Savior from David's line, the One greater than John the Baptist, condemned in fulfilment of the prophets, laid in a tomb, and then raised by God never to see decay. Paul proves the resurrection from Psalm 2 and Psalm 16 and makes it the ground of the gospel offer: through this risen Jesus the forgiveness of sins is proclaimed, and everyone who believes is justified. Christ is both the goal of Israel's history and the Savior of the nations.
study.god: God is the acting subject of the whole story: He chose the fathers, led Israel out with an uplifted arm, gave judges, kings, and David, kept His promise, and raised Jesus from the dead. The mission is His, launched by His Spirit. And His saving purpose was never for Israel alone; He appointed His servant a light for the Gentiles, so that salvation would reach the ends of the earth, and those appointed for eternal life believe.

crossReferences.note: You are My Son; quoted to declare Jesus the risen, enthroned Son of God.
You will not let Your Holy One see decay; the proof that the Messiah must rise, since David died and decayed.
A light for the Gentiles, salvation to the ends of the earth; the commission Paul claims for the mission.
A person is justified by faith in Christ, not by works of the law, unfolding the justification Paul first announces in this sermon.

study.oneStory: Acts follows the ascended Christ driving His gospel outward by the Spirit, and here the mission is formally launched. Paul's sermon shows the whole Old Testament, exodus, judges, David, prophets, converging on Jesus, crucified and risen. The turning to the Gentiles is not a change of plan but the keeping of an ancient promise, that God's salvation would be a light to the nations. From Antioch the word now begins the journey that will not stop until it reaches the ends of the earth.

quiz.q: How did the first missionary journey begin?

options.text: The Holy Spirit set apart Barnabas and Saul as the church worshiped and fasted.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 show the Spirit initiating the mission and the church sending them off.

options.text: The church leaders decided it was strategically wise to send out missionaries.
options.note: The church did commission them, but the decisive initiative came from the Holy Spirit, not human strategy alone.

options.text: Paul and Barnabas set off on their own to explore new territory.
options.note: They were sent by the Spirit and the church, not acting on private initiative.

options.text: Rome commissioned them to spread a new religion.
options.note: The sending was by the Holy Spirit through the church, not by any imperial authority.

quiz.q: What is the center of Paul's sermon at Pisidian Antioch?

options.text: That God raised the crucified Jesus from the dead, never to see decay, as the Scriptures promised.
options.note: The resurrection is the heart of the sermon, proven from Psalm 2 and Psalm 16.

options.text: That Jesus is the promised Savior from David's line.
options.note: True and important, but the sermon's climax is the resurrection that vindicates this Savior.

options.text: That Israel had a long and glorious history.
options.note: The history is the runway; it all leads to the risen Jesus, which is the point.

options.text: That the law of Moses is the path to righteousness.
options.note: Paul says the opposite: the law could not justify, but faith in the risen Christ does.

quiz.q: What does Paul say a person receives through faith in Jesus?

options.text: Forgiveness of sins and justification from everything the law could not justify.
options.note: Verses 38 to 39 offer both forgiveness and a righteous standing through Christ by faith.

options.text: Forgiveness of sins, so the guilt of the past is wiped away.
options.note: Forgiveness is real, but Paul also stresses justification, a full acquittal the law could never give.

options.text: A better ability to keep the law of Moses.
options.note: The point is not improved law-keeping but a justification the law could not provide.

options.text: Nothing until they have proven themselves worthy.
options.note: The gift is offered to everyone who believes, not earned by proving worth.

quiz.q: Why did Paul and Barnabas turn to the Gentiles?

options.text: Because many Jews rejected the word, and Scripture commanded a light for the Gentiles.
options.note: Verses 46 to 47 give both the rejection and the Isaiah commission as reasons.

options.text: Because the Jewish leaders grew jealous and contradicted the message.
options.note: The jealousy is part of the rejection, but Paul also grounds the turn in God's command in Isaiah.

options.text: Because Gentiles were easier to convince than Jews.
options.note: Ease was not the reason; it was rejection by many Jews and the scriptural mandate.

options.text: Because God had given up on Israel entirely.
options.note: Paul preached to the Jew first and never says God has cast off Israel; the gospel remained open to them.

quiz.q: How does David function in the sermon?

options.text: As a pointer to Jesus: David served, died, and decayed, but his greater Son was raised never to decay.
options.note: Verses 36 to 37 contrast David's decay with the risen Christ, fulfilling the promise to David.

options.text: As the model king whom God loved and used.
options.note: David was a man after God's heart, but the sermon uses him mainly to point beyond himself to Christ.

options.text: As proof that faithful kings never really die.
options.note: The opposite: David did die and see decay, which is exactly Paul's point.

options.text: As an example unrelated to Jesus.
options.note: David is directly tied to Jesus, the Savior brought from his descendants.

questions: The mission began while the church was worshiping and fasting, listening for the Spirit. How much room does your life and church leave for listening to God before acting?
Paul preached Israel's whole story as leading to Jesus. How does seeing the Old Testament flow toward Christ deepen the way you read your Bible?
Paul offered forgiveness and justification that the law could never give. Where are you still trying to earn a standing with God that Christ offers you freely?
The Gentiles rejoiced and glorified the word while others were filled with jealousy. Why do you think the same message brings joy to some and resentment to others?
Paul and Barnabas were driven out, yet the disciples were filled with joy and the Spirit. What would it look like for your joy to depend on the word's advance rather than on comfort or acceptance?

translationNotes.note: The testimony about David, a man after My own heart, draws on 1 Samuel 13:14 and Psalm 89:20, presenting David as the God-chosen king whose greater Son is Jesus.
The quotation is Psalm 2:7; the BSB footnote notes the literal today I have begotten You. Paul applies the enthronement of the Son to the resurrection of Jesus.
The holy and sure blessings promised to David render Isaiah 55:3; the BSB footnote gives the literal the holy, the trustworthy of David. The sure mercies of David are secured in the risen Christ.
The warning to scoffers is quoted from Habakkuk 1:5, following the Greek Old Testament; the closing quotation mark stands as a separate element in the source text.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 13 (long chapter, all 52 verses included). Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem or note objects were skipped, keeping only text; elements joined by single spaces. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon verified this session.
